Seek is already implemented to operate on codepoints. I updated the pull request an implemented len and tell as well, but tell is pretty inefficient. I am looking at doing it in larger chunks when possible, something like 21333 which even if all 21333 characters are 3 btyes long, falls under 64000 byes, so it'll nicely fit into the L2 cache of most processors without any problems.

I'm thinking in terms of the tell implementation, a better option than what I did in my updated pull request would be to check the position (in bytes) then seek in chunks, recording each along the way, until the chunk read takes you past the original tell() point. At that point you could back up and read in smaller increments until you hit the byte position. That would be relatively efficient.

There I was checking that my test further down would be valid – if the system as a whole reported no CPU usage (which seemed unlikely), then the `assertAlmostEqual` against `0` could spuriously pass even if it weren't using `my_procfs`. 

It's using `assertNotAlmostEqual` because the cpu times are reported as floats, and in the spurious pass case it might be that the system reported e.g. `0.00000001` CPU usage twice – which could be both `almostEqual` and `notEqual` to 0, providing a narrow path along which the test might pass even if the underlying functionality was broken.

It's a holdover from an older version of the test, though, since I now change `my_procfs` and ensure I can read that back, so I can remove it if you would like.

while this solution is quite good and handles exceptions with a two-phases middleware (like the built-in Django cache middleware), it doesn't cover errors raised in other middlewares. Suppose you have this use case:
```python
class MiddlewareBlast(object):
    def process_request(self, request):
        1 / 0  # i.e. a critical failure while asking data to another service

# in your settings
MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES = [
    'ddtrace.contrib.django.TraceMiddleware',
    'tests.contrib.django.app.middlewares.CatchExceptionMiddleware',
    'tests.contrib.django.app.middlewares.MiddlewareBlast',
    'ddtrace.contrib.django.TraceExceptionMiddleware',
]
```
What happens is that our `TraceExceptionMiddleware` isn't called because the chain is short-circuited immediately. This results in a full trace where a 500 is reported but the `request_span.get_tag('error.stack')` is empty. Adding this extra middleware covers only the case where:
* there is an exception in a view
* a middleware returns a response before hitting the original `TraceMiddleware`.

We may think to handle that differently later on, like wrapping all registered middlewares here: https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/handlers/base.py#L25-L35 so that a second middleware is not required. Also, this would provide even more details about the time spent on Django / custom middlewares (this info is actually missing). Though it's more work and it could be a second step.

@scottpurdy, I spent a bit more time looking into this. There are two parallel sources of information that represent synapses:
1. `InSynapse` objects inside Segments. Each `InSynapse` object contains a cell index value of the source cell that connects to this synapse.
2. `OutSynapse` objects in `cells4::_outSynapses`. Each `OutSynapse` contains the cell and segment indexes that the referring cell connects to.

The parallel info universes exist for optimization, apparently. There is also a method that's used for consistency checks between the two info universes: `Cells4::invariants`.

I examined the code where `_outSynapses` is modified, including the callers of such code, and concluded that `_outSynapses` is a "shadow" data structure, while the Synapses contained in Segment objects are the primary ground truth. At least, that's how it appears. The pattern that I've observe has been to add/remove synapses inside Segment(s) and then follow up with `addOutSynapses`, `eraseOutSynapses`, or `rebuildOutSynapses` to update the "shadow" `_outSynapses`.

If this assessment is correct, then it should be safe to assume that this TODO is misguided given the current state of the code. The synapses inside segments should be valid on entry to this code path and they are not modified by this block of code within this loop.

Trunk is the initial section (so no segment and no point). So, we can use any of the following, as long as the definitions are clear!
trunk_ (elevation, azimuth, direction)
tree_ (elevation, azimuth, direction)
initial_ (elevation, azimuth, direction)

You can go for a fully-general proxy, a la [zope.proxy](http://zopeproxy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/narr.html), but the extra complexity isn't worth the trouble IMO.

The proxy could [define `__getattr__`, and delegate to the proxied object](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.proxy/blob/master/src/zope/proxy/__init__.py#L112).

In the end, the only "smell" to me is the whitelist of proxied attributes / methods;  other than that, copying the bound methods into the proxy seems very straightforward, compared to hooking `__getattr__` (or worse, [`__getattribute__`](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.proxy/blob/master/src/zope/proxy/__init__.py#L94)).

Rather than a factory for instantiating all three of these, I think that the clearest implementation at this point would be to have this be a graph of `Subsystems`. Subsystems are like `Tasks`, in that they can have options. They can also have dependencies on other Subsystems. Finally, Tasks can have dependencies on Subsystems.

Concretely: `JunitRun` already has a dependency on two subsystems:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/junit_run.py#L193-L195
...and I'm suggesting that Jacoco and Cobertura become Subsystems (ie, subclassing Subsystem), and that JunitRun should depend on them. Then, the "use a coverage-processor" option would stay in JunitRun, but would select which Subsystem to use.

Getting multiple failed roots here doesn't mean multiple failures, necessarily. Because a `Throw` in the graph with two "parent" nodes will fail both parents with the same `Throw`.

Deduping by the string value of the throw might work, but the real right way would be to find the lowest point in the graph, which is where the `Throw` originated (as trace does). Deduping is probably fine, but executing a different native method might be cleaner?

Calling `getinfo` will raise a `ResourceNotFound` as expected, whereas if you were to use `isdir` you would need one call to check for the existence of a resource, and a second call to check that the resource is a directory.

Since the base implementation uses `getinfo` as a base for `isdir`, `isfile` and `exist`, it will result in only one call to `getinfo` to do as such but two calls otherwise.

I checked by doing this:
```python
>>> import zipfile
>>> with zipfile.ZipFile('test.zip', 'w') as z:
...    info = zipfile.ZipInfo('test.txt')
...    z.writestr(info, '')
```
and then 
```
$ zipinfo test.zip
Archive:  test.zip
Zip file size: 114 bytes, number of entries: 1
?rw-------  2.0 unx        0 b- stor 80-Jan-01 00:00 test.txt
1 file, 0 bytes uncompressed, 0 bytes compressed:  0.0%
```

So I think actually having external attr as `0` means that there are no permission defined, which will result in `0o600` being applied when extracting on Unix. So it may be a better idea to only add the `access/permissions` info if external attr is the permissions are defined, and let the user do what he wants on no permissions.

Permissions of zip objects are a mess, since zip file don't store the uid/gid of the file owners, so even with permissions mode they are rendered pretty useless.
